segments.s154-g33: 'ترا' here is the old-fashioned ordering of the 2nd-person marker + object clitic (ت + را) placed before the verb for emphasis — it marks 'you' as the one who must act. 'رفت' is the verb 'go' (root رفتن) used in a poetic/inverted word order; 'باید' is the modal 'must/it is necessary' that governs the following action. 'به نزدیک او' is a prepositional phrase ('به' + noun 'نزدیک' used as 'near' + pronoun 'او' 'him') meaning 'to/near him'. Together: 'You must go near him.'
segments.s154-g34: 'درفشان' is used as a participial/adjectival element meaning 'shining, made to shine' (rather than the unrelated noun 'standard‑bearer' here). 'کنی' is the 2nd‑person singular subjunctive/present of 'کردن' ('you make/you cause'), the verb that completes the necessity expressed by 'باید' earlier. 'جان تاریک او' is a genitive phrase with noun + adjective + pronoun ('the dark soul of him'), i.e. 'his dark soul.' The verb phrase literally 'درفشان کنی جان تاریک او' = 'you cause his dark soul to shine.'
segments.s154-g17: بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him' (be + 3rd-person clitic) placed before the verb; گفت is the past tense 'said' (here used as a reporting verb) though the provided sense-list does not give the verbal sense; کاووس is the speaker's name in the nominative; کز (= که + ز) here functions as the fused conjunction/preposition 'that/from' and begins the subordinate clause 'that/from the assembly...'; انجمن is the noun 'assembly/meeting' governed by کز.
segments.s154-g18: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'; زنده ماند is the verb phrase 'remain alive / survive' (زنده adjective + ماند, imperfect/past-stem meaning 'remain' or 'survive'); چنان is a demonstrative/adverb 'such' or 'so' qualifying what follows; پیلتن is a place-name (here the endpoint or comparison). The clause as a whole is the protasis: 'if he remains alive (after the assembly) ...'.
segments.s154-g19: شود is the present/subjunctive/future form of شدن 'become / will be' used here to state a future or inevitable consequence; پشت رستم means 'the backing/support of Rostam' (پشت as 'support/protection' with the name رستم in genitive relationship); به نیرو is an agent/means phrase 'by/with force'; ترا is the object 'you' expressed by the enclitic ت plus the object marker را (split in tokens). The word order places the future verb first for emphasis, then the noun phrase describing what will happen to 'you'.
segments.s154-g20: هلاک آورد is a verb phrase where هلاک is 'ruin/destruction' used as the object/theme and آورد (from آوردن) 'bring/cause' is the verb: 'bring about destruction'; بی‌گمانی is an adverb 'undoubtedly/doubtless' modifying the verb; مرا and the following را appear as clitics marking the experiencer/object — the tokenization in the data is nonstandard, but grammatically this part emphasizes certainty and the affected party. Overall: 'he will certainly bring (your) destruction.'
segments.s154-g21: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'; یک زمان 'at one time/if ever' gives a temporal sense ('if at any time'); ز (here fused with او as زو) is the preposition 'from' marking source ('from him'); به من marks the recipient 'to me'; بد رسد is the verb phrase 'evil/bad befalls' (بد adjective + رسد 'happen, befall') — together: 'if at any time harm from him reaches me.' Note: tokenization shows ز and و separately, but functionally this is 'زو' = 'from him'.
segments.s154-g22: نسازیم is negative + present/subjunctive/optative form (ن + سازیم) meaning 'we shall/must not (do) otherwise' or 'we will not make/offer (except)'; پاداش او is 'his reward/recompense' (پاداش + pronoun او); جز به بد is a limiting/exclusive phrase 'except with evil' or 'only by harm' (جز = except, به بد = by bad). The clause states the speaker's intended recompense: 'we will give him no reward except ill.'
segments.s154-g23: کجا is the interrogative 'where'; گنجد is the present/subjunctive form of گنجیدن 'fit, be contained' (used here rhetorically); او is 'he'; در جهان فراخ is a locative phrase 'in the broad/spacious world' describing the scope. The syntactic order is interrogative + verb + subject + locative complement: 'Where can he be contained in the spacious world?'
segments.s154-g24: بدان here is a composite: ب- as a prepositional prefix 'in/to' (the tokenization splits it) combined with دان (the dataset tokenization is irregular), introducing a list of attributes; فر 'farr' is 'royal glory/radiance'; و ... و links the coordinated nouns 'that glory and that height and that mane and horn' — آن marks each item as 'that'; برز 'height/exaltation' and یال 'mane' and شاخ 'horn' are coordinate nouns enumerating the qualities in which he could not be contained. This is a poetic accumulation of epithets.
segments.s154-g1: This clause contains the verb گفت (said) with indirect object marked by به + proper name: به گودرز گفت = 'said to Goudarz'. آن زمان is a temporal adverbial 'at that time/then' placed before the vocative appositive پهلوان ('O hero' / 'the hero'). The subject is omitted (typical in Persian past narration).
segments.s154-g2: This is the content of what was said. کز here introduces (addressing) the person being spoken to—'you who...'—leading into the imperative برو 'go'. ایدر = 'from here / hence', زود = adverb 'quickly/soon', and روشن‌روان is an epithet 'bright‑souled / cheerful' vocatively applied to the addressee.
segments.s154-g3: پیامی 'a message' is the object; ز من marks the source 'from me'; پیش کاووس = 'before/in the presence of Kavus' (i.e. to Kavus). The postposition بر here functions as a directional marker 'to/toward' linking this phrase to the verb that follows.
segments.s154-g4: بگویش is an imperative 'tell/say to him' (بگو + enclitic -ش for 'to him'); که introduces a content clause. مارا چه آمد به سر is an idiomatic clause meaning 'what has happened to us / what befell us'—آمد (happened/occurred) + به سر (literally 'to the head', here idiomatic 'befall').
segments.s154-g5: به here means 'by/with' (instrumental) marking the means: 'with a dagger'. جگرگاه 'the heart/innermost part' is the affected target, and پور دلیر is an appositive 'the brave son' (پور = 'son/descendant', دلیر = 'brave'). Word order is idiomatic poetic grouping of instrument + target + epithet.
segments.s154-g6: دریدم is the past first‑person 'I tore/pierced'. که introduces the result clause 'so that / and thus' followed by رستم (subject) and مماناد (past negative: 'did not remain'); دیر is an adverbial 'long' indicating duration: 'Rostam did not remain long.'
segments.s154-g7: گرت is a conditional 'if' (contraction of 'اگر تو'); یادست functions as 'there is remembrance' / 'you remember' and is governed by the conditional. هیچ here is indefinite ('any/at all') modifying یاد; کردار من = 'my deeds'. The whole phrase: 'If you have any memory of my deeds...'.
segments.s154-g8: رنجه کن is an imperative construction ('do be troubled' → 'show pity / take trouble'); یکی here = 'once/for once'; دل به تیمار من is the object phrase 'put your heart to caring for me' (دل = heart, به تیمار = into care/concern, من = me). Together it pleads 'for once trouble your heart for me.'
segments.s154-g9: ازان = a compound of از + آن, 'of/from that' pointing back to a thing previously mentioned; نوشدارو is a compound noun meaning a restorative/medicine; که is a relative pronoun 'that/which' introducing a relative clause; در گنج is a prepositional phrase 'in (the) treasury' (در + گنج); تست is the enclitic 2nd‑person possessive/verb fusion here indicating 'is (of) you' or 'in your possession' (classical contraction of تو + است). Together the segment names the thing: 'that restorative which is in your treasury.'
segments.s154-g10: کجا is an interrogative 'where' (introducing the question about location or possibility); خستگان (pl.) 'the weary' is the logical object; را is the Persian definite/object marker attached to the object; کند is the 3rd‑person singular present/future of کردن 'to do/make' (here causative: 'would make'); تن 'body' is the thing to be made whole; درست is an adjective 'sound/proper/whole' modifying تن. Word order is typical Persian V‑final for the verb کند, with the object phrase before it.
segments.s154-g11: به نزدیک من is a locative phrase 'near me / close to me' (به + نزدیک + من). با یکی جام می is a noun phrase 'with a single cup (of wine)': با here marks accompaniment/means, یکی functions as an indefinite 'one/a certain', جام 'cup' and می is the liquid 'wine' (mi). The clause is elliptical/imperative in tone (the verb 'bring/place' is implied by context).
segments.s154-g12: سزد‌گر is a conditional/concessive particle/phrase 'if it be fitting / if you deem it right' (sazd‑gar); فرستی is 2nd‑person singular present/subjunctive or imperative of فرستادن 'to send' ('you send' / 'send'); هم‌اکنون 'right now / at once' is an adverb; به پی is a prepositional phrase 'in pursuit / after (him/them)'. The construction expresses a conditional instruction: 'If you think it fitting, send (someone) at once in pursuit.'
segments.s154-g13: مگر here introduces a negative/exclusive condition 'unless' (it sets up an exception); کاو in this classical/poetic context is an archaic form (not the common verb 'to dig' in modern usage) and functions here as a pronoun/particle referring to 'his/that' (see note) — its precise lexical identity is archaic; ببخت appears as a form referring to 'fortune/luck' (classical usage) and تو 'you' marks toward whom the fortune pertains; بهتر شود is a verbal clause 'becomes better'. Overall this segment says 'unless his fortune for you becomes better.'
segments.s154-g14: چو here functions as a conditional 'if' (classical 'چو' = 'if/when'); من is the 1st‑person subject 'I'; پیش تخت تو is a locative/relational phrase 'in the presence of / before your throne' (پیش + تخت + تو); کهتر شود is a verb phrase 'become lesser/inferior' (کهتر 'inferior' + شود 'become'). Grammatically it is the protasis/consequence clause linked to the previous condition.
segments.s154-g15: بیامد is the simple past 'came' (narrative); سپهبد is the title 'spahbed' (a high military rank) and بکردار is an epithet 'of good conduct/virtuous'; باد here is the optative particle 'may (he be)' used as a blessing/wish attached to the epithet (classical poetic usage). The clause therefore narrates the arrival and appends a conventional wish about his character.
segments.s154-g16: به کاووس is a prepositional phrase 'to Kavus' (به + proper name); یکسر is an adverb 'at once/entirely' modifying the delivery; پیامش is 'his message' (پیام + enclitic ش = his); بداد here is the past tense verb 'gave' / 'delivered' (classical form of دادن). The clause means 'he immediately gave/delivered his message to Kavus.'
segments.s154-g25: شنیدی is 2nd-person past/perfect ‘you heard’ (verb first in this clause). که introduces reported speech (‘that’). او is the reported-subject ‘he’. گفت is the past verb ‘said’ introducing the quoted question that follows. کاووس کیست is an embedded question: کاووس (proper name) + کیست, where کی is the interrogative ‘who’ and -ست is the archaic/past-present copula marking ‘is’ (here forming ‘who is Kavus’).
segments.s154-g26: گر is the conditional conjunction ‘if’. او is the subject ‘he’. شهریارست is a fused form with شهریار ‘king’ + -ست (copula) meaning ‘is king’ (predicate). پس is the result/so/then linking the conditional to the following question. طوس کیست repeats the question pattern: طوس (proper name, here the hero Tous) + کی (‘who’) + -ست ‘is’, yielding ‘then who is Tous?’
segments.s154-g27: کجا is the interrogative ‘where’ placed frontally. باشد is a subjunctive/optative form of ‘to be’ here carrying the sense ‘would be’ in a rhetorical question. او is the subject ‘he’. پیش تختم is a prepositional phrase ‘before/in the presence of my throne’ (پیش + تختم, with تختم ‘my throne’). به پای is another locative phrase ‘at the foot (of)’; word order is rhetorical and somewhat compressed, typical of poetic Persian.
segments.s154-g28: Again کجا opens the question. راند is the past form ‘drove’ (or ‘would drive’ in the rhetorical sense). او is the actor ‘he’ (object or person being driven may be implicit). زیر is the preposition ‘under/beneath’. فر (here) means the royal radiance or ‘glory’ and همای names the mythic bird (the Simurgh) — together فر همای = ‘the glory of the Simurgh’. The phrase thus asks where he would drive (or thrust) someone under that splendour.
segments.s154-g29: چو is the temporal conjunction ‘when’. بشنید is the past tense ‘he heard’. گودرز is the subject name (Goudarz). برگشت is the past verb ‘returned/turned back’. زود is an adverb ‘soon/quickly’ modifying برگشت and placed after the verb (poetic word order).
segments.s154-g30: بر + رستم forms the verb phrase ‘came to/onto Rostam’ (بر here = toward/to). آمد is the past ‘came’. به کردار is the preposition ‘in the manner of’ (به + کردار ‘manner/behavior’), and دود (‘smoke’) completes the simile: ‘came to Rostam in the manner of smoke’ (i.e., swiftly or stealthily).
segments.s154-g31: بدو is the dative/enclitic-like phrase ‘to him’. گفت is the past ‘said’. خوی بد شهریار is a noun phrase: خوی ‘disposition/temper’ + بد ‘bad’ + شهریار ‘of the king’ (genitive relationship expressed by adjective order), yielding ‘the king’s bad temper/ disposition’. The whole chunk is ‘he said to him: “the king’s bad temper…”’
segments.s154-g32: درختیست is a verbless/predicative construction ‘(it) is a tree’ (درخت + -یست/copula). خنگی is an adjective/noun meaning ‘stupid/foolish’ describing that tree. همیشه is the adverb ‘always’. به بار here is an idiomatic locution meaning ‘bearing fruit’ (به بار آمدن = to bear fruit); literally ‘to/at load’ where بار = ‘load/fruit’. The line says the king’s bad temper is a foolish tree that always bears fruit (i.e., yields consequences).

sentences.s154-s17: You must go close to him and make his dark soul shine.
sentences.s154-s9: Kavus said to him that, if he should survive the assembly, (it would be) to Piltan (thus).
sentences.s154-s10: Rostam's backing will be set against you with force; he will bring about your destruction, doubtless.
sentences.s154-s11: If at any time ill will comes to me from him, we will repay him with nothing but harm.
sentences.s154-s12: Where would he fit in the wide world — in that glory and that height and that mane and horn?
sentences.s154-s1: At that time the hero said to Goudarz, "Go from here quickly, O bright‑souled one."
sentences.s154-s2: Take a message from me to Kavus; tell him what has befallen us.
sentences.s154-s3: With a dagger I pierced the heart of the brave son; Rostam did not remain long.
sentences.s154-s4: If you recall any of my deeds, for once take pity—set your heart to caring for me.
sentences.s154-s5: Where is that restorative in your treasury that would make the bodies of the weary whole?
sentences.s154-s6: Place (bring) near me a cup of wine; if you deem it fitting, send (someone) immediately in pursuit.
sentences.s154-s7: Unless his fortune toward you improves, if I should become diminished before your throne.
sentences.s154-s8: The spahbed, virtuous (may he be), came, and at once delivered his message to Kavus.
sentences.s154-s13: Did you hear that he said, “Who is Kavus? If he is king, then who is Tous?”
sentences.s154-s14: Where would he be before my throne at my feet? Where would he drive him beneath the splendour of the royal Simurgh?
sentences.s154-s15: When Goudarz heard, he turned back quickly and came to Rostam like smoke.
sentences.s154-s16: He said to him, 'The king's bad disposition is a foolish tree that is always bearing fruit.'
